responses.strategic_rationale: The plan must navigate four core tensions: data fidelity versus participant safety and cost, enrollment speed versus sample homogeneity, privacy and governance versus external collaboration, and scalable annotation throughput versus human review burden. The six levers below balance these tensions by tuning monitoring depth, recruitment scope, data governance, staffing workflows, automated triage, and facility design. Each lever offers distinct strategic pathways that challenge assumptions about resource limits, data quality requirements, and the trade-off between ecological validity and safety oversight, aiming to produce robust methods validation and phenotyping while preserving ethical standards and operational viability.

levers.name: Monitoring modality mix optimization
levers.consequences: Adjusting the balance between low-burden nightly monitoring and high-fidelity PSG nights changes data richness, participant burden, and operational costs. A higher share of full PSG nights improves event capture and sleep staging accuracy but raises staffing demands and participant strain; this trade-off can constrain enrollment pace if safety thresholds are not met.
options: Expand enhanced nights to all participants on a rotating schedule to maximize data richness.
Limit enhanced nights to cases with flagged events and rely on dry-electrode and mattress sensors for screening.
Introduce automated real-time alarm-based escalation nights triggered by sensor anomalies to balance data yield and safety.
levers.review_lever: Controls safety and ethical oversight vs data throughput and study pace. Weakness: The options fail to consider participant burden and regulatory feasibility.

levers.name: Recruitment strategy flexibility
levers.consequences: Expanding eligibility and recruitment channels increases the eligible pool and can accelerate enrollment, but may raise sample heterogeneity and screening workload. Broader criteria can dilute effect detection but improve generalizability; expanded channels require standardized pre-admission processes to maintain comparability.
options: Broaden eligibility to 18–70 with a standardized safety pre-screen to maintain risk control.
Scale recruitment through regional clinics with uniform pre-admission screening to reduce site heterogeneity.
Implement retention-focused incentives and enhanced participant communication to improve stay completion.
levers.review_lever: Controls enrollment pace vs sample heterogeneity. Weakness: The options fail to consider retention challenges and comparability to planned control group.

levers.name: Data governance and external collaboration strategy
levers.consequences: Relaxed data-sharing policies can enhance external validation and collaboration but require stronger privacy safeguards, consent management, and governance. Stricter controls protect participant privacy but may limit external replication and cross-site validation; governance must balance openness with risk control.
options: Deposit de-identified datasets into public repositories with controlled access.
Restrict sharing to internal university partners with tiered access and formal data-use agreements.
Create a data-use governance board to review external collaboration requests and privacy safeguards.
levers.review_lever: Controls openness of data sharing vs privacy risk management. Weakness: The options fail to consider consent scope across institutions.

levers.name: Staffing and scorer workflow optimization
levers.consequences: Increasing double scoring and cross-training improves reliability and throughput but raises labor costs and potential fatigue. A more versatile workforce can accelerate data processing but requires robust training and supervision to maintain quality.
options: Cross-train technicians to perform collection and initial scoring, expanding versatile staffing.
Institute double-scoring with independent raters and an adjudication round for all events.
Adopt a rotating night-shift model with on-call senior scorer for complex cases.
levers.review_lever: Controls throughput of scoring vs inter-rater reliability. Weakness: The options fail to consider budgetary constraints and fatigue.

levers.name: Event-triage tool development and validation
levers.consequences: Semi-automation can reduce manual review and speed adjudication, but introduces bias risk and requires rigorous validation and ongoing oversight. Early pilot results must be designed to preserve interpretability and human verification to avoid misclassification.
options: Develop and validate a semi-automated event-triage model using retrospective labeled data before live use.
Adopt a transparent rule-based triage approach with predefined criteria and human-in-the-loop verification.
Pilot cross-stream validation comparing tool outputs against dual human scoring across participants.
levers.review_lever: Controls automation of triage vs human oversight. Weakness: The options fail to consider bias and validation requirements.

levers.name: Facility safety and naturalistic environment balance
levers.consequences: Modular room design and flexible safety features preserve ecological validity while ensuring participant safety, but may constrain layout and workflow optimization. Safer environments can limit certain observational dynamics without harming data quality if designed thoughtfully.
options: Implement modular room configurations that can adapt between domestic-like and safety-focused layouts.
Enhance real-time monitoring and emergency response with redundant alarms and cross-trained staff.
Minimize camera footprint to zones with consent while enabling safety-focused video access for flagged events.
levers.review_lever: Controls ecological validity vs safety overhead. Weakness: The options fail to consider regulatory approvals for environment changes.

responses.summary: One critical missing dimension is cross-site external validation to demonstrate generalizability beyond Bonn. Add 'External cross-site validation at a second European site using a harmonized protocol to test generalizability' to Data governance and sharing.

responses.strategic_rationale: The core tensions center on maximizing data richness and validity while preserving participant safety and naturalistic behavior within a small residential unit. Balancing staffing workload, data governance, and retention against the need for high-resolution, externally comparable phenotyping drives a mix of governance, operational, and analytics levers. The proposed levers push toward rigorous scoring, robust data handling, and participant-centered design to enable scalable, ethically compliant long-term study of parasomnias beyond Bonn while preserving data quality and safety.

levers.name: PSG escalation nights protocol formalization
levers.consequences: Formalizing escalation nights increases the likelihood of capturing high-resolution PSG data during flagged periods, supporting robust event characterization. Downstream, this will require tighter scheduling, higher staffing during escalation windows, and clearer criteria for when escalation is triggered, potentially increasing nightly workload during escalation weeks.
options: Institute a structured escalation night protocol featuring full PSG on nights flagged by automated detectors.
Implement a rotating escalation schedule to align high-detail data capture with participant milestones and clinic workload.
Reserve escalation nights for nights with clear event indicators to maximize data yield while limiting routine intrusion.
levers.review_lever: Controls high-resolution PSG escalation nights vs routine nocturnal monitoring. Weakness: The options fail to consider escalation costs and staff workload implications.

levers.name: Residential experience optimization under safety-by-design
levers.consequences: Enhancing room ambiance and safety features supports participant comfort and longer residency, which increases data capture opportunities. Downstream, comfort-driven adjustments may introduce variability in environmental conditions that complicate cross-participant standardization and statistical comparison.
options: Enhance room ambiance and privacy controls to improve participant comfort while maintaining monitoring fidelity.
Adopt a flexible scheduling and meal plan to boost retention without compromising data collection.
Integrate modular environmental adjustments (lighting, sound) that preserve naturalistic sleep but require extra safety monitoring.
levers.review_lever: Controls participant comfort vs data fidelity. Weakness: The options fail to consider impact on data consistency and incident reporting overhead.

levers.name: EEG-BIDS data governance and local storage hardening
levers.consequences: Strengthening the data pipeline with standardized metadata, encryption, and robust local backups reduces risk of data loss and misannotation. Downstream, these controls may slow ad-hoc data exports and require ongoing staff training to maintain compliance.
options: Enforce automated metadata tagging and local encryption to reduce data handling errors.
Establish a local NAS redundancy plan with offline backups to mitigate data loss.
Standardize data de-identification pipelines before export to repositories.
levers.review_lever: Controls local storage resilience vs rapid data sharing. Weakness: The options fail to consider cross-site data harmonization timelines.

levers.name: Dual-scorer calibration and scoring efficiency
levers.consequences: Regular calibration sustains high inter-rater reliability and reduces bottlenecks in data review, supporting timely analyses. Downstream, calibration demand and clearer guidelines increase training needs and may slow early data turnover.
options: Schedule regular inter-rater calibration sessions to maintain scoring reliability.
Implement a blinded adjudication workflow with independent raters for all nights.
Pilot a semi-automated scoring assistant to support raters without replacing human judgment.
levers.review_lever: Controls inter-rater reliability vs. review throughput. Weakness: The options fail to consider training resource demands and annotation backlog.

levers.name: Artifact management and sensor reliability enhancement
levers.consequences: Improved sensor reliability reduces data gaps and improves overall data integrity, enabling more robust phenotyping. Downstream, maintenance activity and downtime may increase staffing demands and require buffer capacity.
options: Institute routine sensor calibration and redundancy checks for night shifts.
Deploy protocol for rapid replacement of faulty sensors to minimize data gaps.
Adopt a lightweight participant-logged artifact flag process to aid review.
levers.review_lever: Controls data quality vs operational disruption. Weakness: The options fail to consider replacement lead times and supply chain constraints.

levers.name: Informed consent optimization and collateral history integration
levers.consequences: Integrating collateral history into the consent process improves eligibility screening and participant understanding, potentially increasing enrollment quality. Downstream, broader consent scopes may extend pre-admission timelines and require more extensive governance oversight.
options: Integrate structured collateral history gathering into the consent workflow to improve eligibility screening.
Offer tiered consent options for different data uses to align with participant comfort.
Provide a pre-admission educational session to reduce dropouts during enrollment.
levers.review_lever: Controls consent breadth vs enrollment stability. Weakness: The options fail to consider regulatory review timelines and ethics gatekeeping.

responses.summary: Critical missing dimension: external validation readiness and cross-site replication alignment to ensure generalizability beyond the Bonn facility. Add 'Cross-site external validation protocol and replication blueprint integrated into EEG-BIDS governance to formalize external collaborations and data harmonization' to EEG-BIDS data governance and local storage hardening.

responses.strategic_rationale: The project confronts core tensions around data fidelity and participant burden, safety and cost, privacy versus data utility, recruitment pace versus safety, data quality versus staffing load, and controlled expansion versus long-term sustainability. The proposed levers target sensing architecture, overnight staffing, data governance, recruitment strategy, annotation quality, and phased capacity growth to navigate these trade-offs. By binding operational choices to data quality and safety milestones, the plan aims to sustain high-quality longitudinal phenotyping and tools development within a feasible three-year window while maintaining ethical and regulatory alignment.

levers.name: Sensing modality hierarchy reconfiguration
levers.consequences: Adopting a tiered sensing scheme shifts emphasis toward comfortable, low-burden streams on routine nights, which can increase participant retention and data yield from naturalistic nights. A downstream implication is the need for robust alerting and a clear escalation protocol to avoid missed events during early nights when high-resolution data are absent.
options: Default to dry-electrode EEG headbands and mattress sensors for routine nights to optimize comfort and data yield.
Activate a rapid escalation to full PSG on any suspected parasomnia cue to maximize diagnostic precision.
Pilot adaptive weighting across streams based on prior data quality to balance data richness with participant burden.
levers.review_lever: Controls data fidelity vs. participant burden. Weakness: The options fail to consider escalation timing and potential gaps in event capture during early nights.

levers.name: Staffing model for night coverage and incident response
levers.consequences: Reconfiguring staffing affects incident response speed and safety during overnight observations and admission peaks; it can reduce fatigue if planned well but may raise payroll costs and training needs, potentially slowing expansion.
options: Maintain a fixed multi-shift coverage plan to ensure rapid incident response across all eight suites.
Implement on-call escalation for flagged events to reduce overnight staffing costs.
Cross-train technicians to perform multiple roles and share load during peak weeks.
levers.review_lever: Controls safety of overnight operations vs. cost efficiency. Weakness: The options fail to consider staff burnout and long-term retention.

levers.name: Privacy-preserving data sharing and de-identification strategy
levers.consequences: Enables compliant data sharing while protecting participant confidentiality; may reduce granularity and limit certain analyses, requiring clear governance and robust de-identification, which can increase processing time and coordination.
options: De-identify datasets with exhaustive removal of direct identifiers before any external sharing.
Publish aggregated metrics with controlled access to raw signals under governance.
Enforce tiered data-sharing agreements that grant clinicians access within the university network.
levers.review_lever: Controls privacy protection vs. data utility. Weakness: The options fail to consider the risks of re-identification through multi-modal streams.

levers.name: Recruitment funnel optimization under safety constraints
levers.consequences: Streamlines candidate intake and increases enrollment efficiency without compromising safety; however, stricter exclusions may reduce the eligible pool and slow recruitment, affecting project timeline and statistical power.
options: Expand recruitment to regional sleep clinics to widen the eligible pool.
Tighten pre-screening with collateral histories to reduce in-lab dropouts.
Offer modular participation with non-overnight components to increase enrollment reach.
levers.review_lever: Controls recruitment pace vs. safety risk. Weakness: The options fail to consider long-term participant diversity and retention across the cohort.

levers.name: Data quality assurance workflow for multi-stream annotation
levers.consequences: Strengthens inter-rater reliability and data integrity across streams, enabling robust longitudinal phenotyping; but it increases manual review load and staff time, potentially slowing turn-around on event adjudication.
options: Implement double-scoring on all flagged events with adjudication by a senior scorer.
Introduce semi-automated flagging to reduce manual review load while preserving accuracy.
Schedule regular calibration sessions and ongoing quality audits to align scorers.
levers.review_lever: Controls scoring accuracy vs. resource use. Weakness: The options fail to consider potential biases in scorer training and drift over time.

levers.name: Phased expansion threshold for suite capacity
levers.consequences: Guides scalable growth aligned to data quality and safety outcomes; delays expansion may slow accrual and discoveries, while premature scaling can strain safety, staffing, and regulatory compliance.
options: Proceed to add more suites once data quality metrics meet predefined benchmarks.
Delay expansion to prioritize data maturation and staffing efficiency.
Implement a modular expansion where additional suites are rented temporarily during peak enrollment.
levers.review_lever: Controls expansion timing vs. capital risk. Weakness: The options fail to consider long-term maintenance of the facility and ongoing safety training as capacity grows.

responses.summary: One critical missing dimension is regulatory and ethics governance for ongoing expansion and cross-site data sharing, including consent management and cross-institution data-use controls. Without formal governance, scaling the residential unit risks non-compliance and inconsistent data handling across modalities. Add 'Develop a formal consent re-consent protocol and governance framework for cross-site data sharing and video retention, with explicit opt-in/opt-out options' to Privacy-preserving data sharing and de-identification strategy. This addition would strengthen participant autonomy and data integrity as capacity grows, ensuring sustainable expansion within ethical boundaries.

system_prompt:
You are an expert strategic analyst. Generate solution space parameters following these directives:

1. **Output Requirements**
   - You must generate 5 to 7 levers per response.
   - Each lever's `options` field must contain exactly 3 qualitative strategic choices as plain strings.

2. **Lever Quality Standards**
   - Consequences: describe the direct effect of pulling this lever, then at least one downstream implication or trade-off. Be concise and grounded — only cite specific numbers if the project context provides evidence for them. Do not fabricate percentages or cost estimates. Target length: 2–4 sentences.
   - Options MUST:
     • Represent genuinely distinct strategic pathways (not just labels)
     • Include at least one unconventional or non-obvious approach
     • NO prefixes (e.g., "Option A:", "Choice 1:")

3. **Strategic Framing**
   - Name each lever using language drawn directly from the project's own domain — avoid formulaic patterns or repeated prefixes
   - Frame options as complete strategic approaches
   - Ensure levers challenge core project assumptions

4. **Validation Protocols**
   - For `review_lever` (one field, two sentences, in this order):
     "Controls [Tension A] vs. [Tension B]. Weakness: The options fail to consider [specific factor]."
     Both clauses are mandatory in every response, in this exact order, in one field.
   - For `summary`:
     • Identify ONE critical missing dimension
     • Prescribe CONCRETE addition: "Add '[full strategic option]' to [lever]"

5. **Prohibitions**
   - NO prefixes/labels in options (e.g., "Option A:", "Choice 1:")
   - NO generic option labels (e.g., "Optimize X", "Tolerate Y")
   - NO placeholder consequences or bracket-wrapped templates
   - NO fabricated statistics or percentages without evidence from the project context
   - NO marketing language (e.g., "game-changing", "cutting-edge", "revolutionary")

6. **Option Structure**
   - Maintain parallel grammatical structure across options
   - Ensure options are self-contained descriptions
   - Each option should be a concrete, actionable approach — not a vague aspiration
user_prompt:
File 'plan.txt':
Plan:
Establish a 3-year residential longitudinal research unit in Bonn, Germany, for the study of adult NREM parasomnias — sleepwalking, confusional arousals, and sleep terrors — with a secondary exploratory arm for REM sleep behavior disorder under a separate protocol with dedicated neurologic screening, reflecting RBD's distinct clinical trajectory per current AASM guidance. The facility is affiliated with University Hospital Bonn's Department of Epileptology, leveraging its existing long-term video-EEG monitoring culture, and addresses a specific methodological gap: traditional single-night polysomnography rarely captures infrequent parasomnia events, while outpatient actigraphy lacks physiological resolution. The unit houses 8 active sleep suites at launch in a converted residential property in a quiet Bonn neighborhood, renovated for safety (padded corridor edges, restricted-opening windows, silent exterior door alarms, fire safety, acoustic treatment, network cabling, accessibility modifications) while preserving a domestic feel — if the environment is too clinical, naturalistic behavior is suppressed. Physical capacity exists for 12 suites, but expansion is deferred until the pilot proves acceptable data quality, manageable false alarm rates, safe staffing ratios, and usable annotation throughput. Sensing uses a tiered acquisition model: nightly low-burden monitoring via dry-electrode EEG headbands, contact-free mattress sensors, unobtrusively mounted infrared cameras, ambient microphones, and environmental sensors; scheduled enhanced nights with fuller PSG montage including standard scalp EEG, EOG, chin EMG, and leg EMG on the first night, one mid-stay night, and the final night per participant; and escalation nights deploying full PSG after technician-flagged events to capture recurrence at clinical-grade resolution. All streams are time-synchronized and stored in EEG-BIDS-compatible format on a local NAS with nightly encrypted backup to university storage, annotated in real time by the night technician and later scored by two independent raters using AASM criteria with inter-rater reliability reported. Public data sharing is limited to de-identified physiological and sensor data under data use agreements — bedroom video carries severe privacy constraints and is not deposited in public repositories. The scientific program has three explicit aims: Aim 1, establish and operationally validate the residential capture model as safe, ethics-approved, and sustainable; Aim 2, characterize within-person and between-person parasomnia patterns longitudinally, including episode frequency, morphology, triggers, and temporal clustering; Aim 3, develop and benchmark semi-automated event-triage tools that rank probable parasomnia episodes and reduce manual review burden, evaluated against dual human scorer agreement rather than promising autonomous diagnosis.

Participant recruitment targets adults aged 18–65 with NREM parasomnia confirmed through a structured pre-admission pathway: specialist clinical interview, collateral history from bed partner or household member where available, prior or screening polysomnography, and a structured exclusion checklist adjudicated by the PI. Explicit exclusions are nocturnal epilepsy or suspected epilepsy mimics, untreated obstructive sleep apnea, active major substance use disorder, severe psychiatric instability, and wandering or injury risk beyond facility capability. Recruitment channels are the University Hospital Bonn sleep clinic, regional neurologist referrals, and the Deutsche Gesellschaft für Schlafforschung und Schlafmedizin network, with per-night compensation of €80 plus meals. A matched control group provides baseline nocturnal movement, arousal patterns, and false-positive sensor activity under identical residential monitoring conditions, enabling benchmarking of the event-triage tools against normal nocturnal behavior. Event-yield planning assumes that based on the inclusion criterion of at least 2 self-reported episodes per month, approximately 70–80% of admitted participants will produce at least one captured and adjudicated event during a 2–8 week stay; stays may be extended up to 2 additional weeks for participants with zero captured events after the initial period, capped at 10 weeks total, with a protocol ceiling of 20% unproductive admissions before triggering a recruitment-criteria review. The core team is 9 people: a PI who is a board-certified sleep medicine physician, 2 postdoctoral researchers in sleep neurophysiology and computational neuroscience respectively, 3 research technicians rotating night shifts to ensure safe coverage of 8 active suites with adequate margin for event response and annotation, a clinical psychologist specializing in sleep disorders, a data engineer managing the sensor pipeline, and a study coordinator handling recruitment, consent, scheduling, and ethics compliance.

Budget is €3.8 million over 3 years, gated: €1.8M year one covering property lease and renovation at approximately €750K, equipment at approximately €450K, staff hiring accounting for German social insurance contributions and actual overnight coverage, ethics approval, and a pilot cohort of 15–20 participants; €1.2M year two covering full operations with 30–35 participants, the enhanced-night PSG program, first publications, and event-triage algorithm development; €800K year three conditional on demonstrable progress by month 24. Personnel costs are approximately €2M over three years and remaining funds cover participant compensation, independent scoring time, data storage and retention, regulatory and legal work, and contingency. Funding sources are DFG research grant and University of Bonn internal research funding as the primary pillars, with a pharmaceutical industry partnership as optional upside rather than a feasibility requirement — if secured, the academic team retains full publication rights with the pharma partner receiving a 30-day pre-publication comment window only. Success criteria at 36 months: safe residential monitoring operations sustained for at least 24 months, enrollment of 50–70 NREM parasomnia participants plus matched controls, capture of a prespecified minimum number of independently adjudicated parasomnia events sufficient for Aim 2 phenotyping analysis, 2–3 peer-reviewed publications in sleep medicine journals covering methods validation and longitudinal characterization, a benchmarked semi-automated event-triage model with reported sensitivity, specificity, and agreement against dual human scoring, de-identified physiological dataset deposited in a recognized repository, and a submitted phase-two grant proposal for expansion and intervention studies. Failure triggers: if by month 18 the facility has enrolled fewer than 25 participants or captured fewer than 40 adjudicated events, the program undergoes external review. Governance is a 3-member external scientific advisory board reviewing progress every 6 months, with the PI holding operational authority and budget reallocations above €50K requiring board approval. Banned words: AI, blockchain, app, VR, AR, gamification, wearable startup, consumer product, DAO, NFT, metaverse, smart home, autonomous diagnosis, digital therapeutic, SaaS, marketplace, validated (in the context of claiming a mature deployable system). Pick a realistic scenario — this is an early-stage clinical research facility producing methods validation and phenotyping work, not a finished diagnostic platform.
